It appears that the Spot Agreement is intended to cover spot market transactions, including interruptible gas purchases and sales. However, as you noted, it also seems to cover firm purchases, which could potentially overlap with the existing Enfolio Master Firm Purchase/Sale Agreement.

To clarify, the Enfolio Spot Agreement is not intended to replace the existing Firm Agreement. Rather, it is meant to provide a separate framework for spot market transactions. Having both agreements in place is not expected to create any significant overlaps or conflicts, as they serve different purposes.

To avoid any potential confusion, we can include a provision in the Spot Agreement that clearly states its relationship to the Firm Agreement. For example, we could include a clause that states that the Spot Agreement applies only to spot market transactions, and that the Firm Agreement continues to govern firm purchases and sales.

If you are still contemplating your choice for Mayor, please read the

http://www.zwire.com/site/news.cfm?newsid=2543733&BRD=1575&PAG=461&dept_id=1
83552&rfi=6
Endorsement of the Kingwood Observer:

MAYOR - Chris Bell

The mayoral race has been a tough one to call. We have wrestled with this
difficult decision. During interviews and forums, how do we really know who
will be the best leader? The three who came to meet with us would probably
all make a good mayor. For the most part, they agree on the problems which
make up the challenges of the future: air pollution, ground to surface water
conversion, potholes, traffic, equal division of tax dollars, mass transit,
emergency services. They all care about the city.

But how to choose, without the influence of political affiliation? Past
behavior and character. On that basis, Chris Bell is the best candidate.

The Observer has endorsed Bell before, when he was running for council. He
is probably the most intelligent of the three major contenders. He has a
quick wit and readily quips that he has held the three least regarded
professions : journalist, lawyer and politician.

Most importantly, he does not make promises he cannot or will not keep. He
has never lied to the Kingwood voter. He did not vote for annexation - he
was not on council during that acrimonious time.

He supports a tax rebate at the end of the fiscal year, he has a
well-thought out traffic management plan and a three-point plan for
improving the Houston Fire Department resources. He supports a vote on any
expansion to Metro's light rail system, although he wisely does not support
stopping the seven-mile project that is already begun. His focus is the
taxpayer as the customer, and the customer drives the services.

He sees the big picture and has the intelligence to lead wisely. He is not
favored by either political party, a good thing in our opinion, and points
out that in this time of national crisis, leaders who can bring diverse
needs and people together will be the best leader.

Bell's major opponent did vote for annexation. He misled Kingwood citizens,
for nearly 18 months, by saying he had not decided on his annexation vote.
In his Observer interview, he admitted that he traded his yes vote on
annexation to then-mayor Bob Lanier in exchange for his "promise" to provide
equal services before he was ever elected!

This is fishy reasoning. First, equal services are guaranteed by state law,
not by mayoral promises. Second, the promise was more likely support for his
council bid.

He sold us out before we ever knew his name and then mislead us again and
again. Dishonesty is a character flaw, not a behavior.

Elect Chris Bell mayor. He is the breath of fresh air City Hall needs.

# # #



Delia W. Johnson
Prescott Legal Search
3900 Essex Lane, Suite 1110
Houston, Texas 77027
www.prescottlegal.com

	01/08/01 02:04 PM
		 
		 To: "Dobin, Nora (Enron)" <nora.dobin@enron.com>
		 cc: "Harvey, Dean" <dharvey@velaw.com>, "Taylor, Mark (Enron)" 
<mark.taylor@enron.com>
		 Subject: Envera

I did look at the Envera documents, and I believe that they do not present
any new or different issues for DealBench.  I am assuming that the Envera
members will be using the DealBench platform for physical delivedry
transactions.  The discounting and special rates are issues that I assume
DealBench faces all the time, and I believe that there are no issues under
the Robinson-Patman Act relating to that.  Under the Robinson-Patman Act it
is ok to give volume discounts if they are justifiable, and I assume they
would be in this case in light of the other considerations.  Also,
Robinson-Patman only applies to "commodities" and the DealBench services
that are being provided are most likely not commodities within the meaning
of the Robinson-Patman Act.  I have spoken briefly with Alison Smith (one of
our antitrust lawyers) about this, and she is going to take a quick look at
those issues to make sure nothing has happened recently in this area that
would be of concern.  

It is my understanding that I was asked to look at this only from
DealBench's standpoint.  There are some EnronOnline issues and issues under
the business opportunity agreement provisions.  I have relayed comments on
the latter to Phillips Nazro of our firm (who is working on the matter on
Enron's behalf and asked me about them).

I think that the EnronOnline issues ought to be obvious to Mark Taylor. Just
in case, I am sending a copy of this to Mark, because I do think that Mark
ought to focus on the words in Section 8.1 "if and to the extent that Enron
can place a matching trade via Enron Online"  Does that language mean that
Enron will be performing a clearing function?  If the answer is yes, there
may be some questions that need to be answered under the Commodity Futures
Modernization Act orf 2000, which I have not even attempted to answer.  I
assume Mark is aware of this, but just in case I'm sending him a copy of
this message.

Subject: RE: Revised DealBench Site License Agreement


Nora,

Per my weekend voicemail, we have reviewed your changes to the Site License 
Agreement and Services Agreement, and have only the following minor comments:

Services Agreement

1.  The reference in Section 4 on indemnification in the first sentence, 
second line, should be to "Net Works", not "DealBench".

You are correct. 

Please note that regarding Section 1, counsel to Net Works has requested more 
specificity of Net Works' duties regarding identification of potential 
bidders  1(a)(i) and bid analysis 1(b)(iii). Accordingly, after consulting 
with the business team, I suggest the following:

For 1(a)(i): assist the Seneca Entities in contacting potential bidders 
identified by the Seneca Entities and if Net Works elects to identify 
potential    bidders, those identified by Net Works;

For 1(b)(iii):  assist the Seneca Entities in analyzing bids for Portfolios 
received on or before September 8, 2000 by reporting bid information in an    
organized manner, such as preparing a factual comparison of bid information; 
and

add new 1(b)(iv): assist the Seneca Entities in responding to bid offers for 
the Portfolios received on or before September 8, 2000.


Site License

1.  Date:  The Services Agreement is dated July 1, and the Site License July 
17.  Is this difference intentional?  The date is not so important except for 
the fact that there are cross-references between the agreements, and 
technically speaking, if they are dated on different dates, then references 
in the earlier agreement (as in the recitals) may cause unintentional busts.

It is my understanding that the Pre-Solicitation Period began before the 
effectiveness of the Site License Agreement.  Subject to confirmation from 
the DealBench team, I do not believe having both Agreements dated as of July 
1, 2000 is a problem.

2. Seneca Address For Notice:  c/o EN-BT Delaware, Inc., 51 W. 52nd St, New 
York, NY 10019, Attn:  Brian J. McGuire.

Change will be made.

3.  Exhibit C, Section 1.  We accept the revised payment structure, but would 
prefer that the $900,000 payment be due on October 2, 2000, rather than the 
"earlier of" construct now included.

Ok.

4.  Exhibit C, Section 3.  Please explain the added language "except for 
video-related costs and expenses", which could be read to mean that either 
such services will not be invoiced (though they are outside the scope of 
services), or that they are expressly within the scope of services.  In any 
event, and to prevent any future confusion on what are/are not extraordinary 
expenses, we would like a proviso to this section that Deal Bench will not 
incur any extraordinary expenses without Seneca's
prior written consent, in Seneca's sole discretion.  If DealBench believes a 
service is necessary, but Seneca doesn't want to pay, then it is Seneca's 
loss, and Deal Bench has satisfied its duties by raising the subject.

The DealBench team advises that although up and downloading of videos are 
included in the Services, video-related costs and expenses will be extra.

Well, that's all.  I think that since the site license is in your system, it 
would be sensible for you to prepare the execution copy.  Likewise, I will 
prepare the execution copy of the services agreement.  Please let me know 
what you think.

This approach makes sense.  Thanks for your help.

Regards,

Scott Kimpel

Based on your description, I understand the proposal to be a licensing agreement between Enron and Natsource for a customized version of EnronOnline. Here are some additional details to consider including in the term sheet:

* A description of the specific features and functionalities of the customized EnronOnline application to be licensed to Natsource
* The terms of the one-year license, including the $200,000 fee payable upon delivery of the application
* Any warranties or representations to be made by Enron regarding the application, and any limitations on liability
* Provisions for termination or cancellation of the agreement, including any notice requirements or penalties
* Any confidentiality or non-disclosure obligations applicable to Natsource with respect to the EnronOnline technology

Let me know if these are the right lines to be thinking along, or if there's anything else we should be considering.

Best,
Mark

Subject: Urgent: NYMEX Trading Information - September 14, 2001

Dear All,

As you are aware, the events of September 11, 2001, have had a significant impact on the global financial markets, including the NYMEX energy trading market. As we continue to assess the situation, we wanted to provide you with some important information regarding trading on the NYMEX.

Firstly, we would like to inform you that the NYMEX exchange is still operational, and trading is continuing as normal. However, we understand that the current market conditions are highly volatile, and we urge you to exercise caution when trading.

In light of the recent events, we have seen a significant increase in trading activity, particularly in the crude oil and natural gas contracts. We want to assure you that the NYMEX clearinghouse is working closely with the industry to ensure that all trades are being properly cleared and settled.

We also want to remind you that the NYMEX has implemented various measures to help manage the current market volatility

Hello Kimberly and Emily, 
I agree that the Euro provisions should be separated from the other group of proposed annexes to the protocol because the Euro subject matter relates to practical issues which must be addressed by the Jan. 1 deadline.  The majority of the other annexes relate to substantive and material changes to the way many existing provisions of the 1992 Master Agreement work (except for, among other things, the Force Majeure provisions which are mostly not addressed in the 1992 Master Agreement. . . . . a possible reason for separating the Force Majeure annex from the rest).  As far as the annexes which would make substantive changes to current provisions of the 1992 agreement, I understand from your email that ISDA is now proposing to role them into a "new" master agreement rather than issuing them in protocol form.  Would it not be a good litmus test to judge the acceptance by the ISDA membership (and otherwise) of each of the proposed annexes by issuing them in protocol form in order to see who and how many sign up for them?
Thank you. 
Best regards, 
Alec L. Cohen

From: Breda Walsh [mailto:Breda.Walsh@ISDA.org]
To: Alec L. Cohen
Cc: Kimberly Summe; Emily Portman
Subject: Re: Proposed Annexes to the 1992 Master Agreement

Dear Alec,

Thank you for your email and for your thoughts on the proposed annexes to the 1992 Master Agreement. We appreciate your agreement that the Euro provisions should be separated from the other proposed annexes due to the urgency of addressing the practical issues related to the Euro by the January 1 deadline.

Regarding your suggestion to issue the proposed annexes in protocol form as a litmus test to gauge the acceptance of the ISDA membership, we understand your reasoning. However, we are concerned that issuing multiple protocols simultaneously could lead to confusion among our members and potentially dilute the effectiveness of the protocol process.

As you are aware, we are considering incorporating some of the proposed annexes into a new master agreement, as you mentioned. We believe this approach will provide a more streamlined and cohesive document for our members. We will take your suggestion into consideration as we move forward with the project.

Best regards,
Breda Walsh

From: Craig Childers [mailto: 
To: Taylor, Michael E
Cc: Lisa Kasper
Subject: Re: NEVCO's Gimzo and ZEV credits

Hi Michael,

Thanks for reaching out to us. Lisa forwarded your email to me, and I'm happy to help. To answer your question, the California Air Resources Board (CARB) has been discussing the possibility of allowing three-wheeled vehicles like NEVCO's Gimzo to earn ZEV credits.

As of now, there is no official decision, but we are expecting a ruling in the coming months. If approved, the credits will likely be retroactive to a certain date, which will be specified by CARB.

I'll keep you updated on any developments. In the meantime, if you have any further questions, feel free to reach out.

Best regards,
Craig Childers

The official rules are as follows:

1. The lines for all games other than Thursdays will be Danny Sheridan's from the Friday edition of the USA Today, for the Thursday games the lines will also be Danny 	Sheridan's but from the Thursday edition of the USA Today.  These are the only games you can choose from.

2. You must pick 5 different games a week against the spread in rule # 1 and assign priorities of 5,4,3,2,1.  In other words the game you are most sure of you would assign 	a 5 priority, and least sure of assign a 1 priority.  The priority selections of the games you win are totalled up to determine your weekly score.  For example if your 	priority 4 and priority 2 game cover the spread for the week and the 5,3, and 1 do not then you will have a weekly score of 6 (4 + 2).

3. All picks must be in by 2:00pm on Thursday for Thursday games and 2:00pm on Friday for weekend games.  Send your selections to

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Here is the continuation of the text:

...either me or Derek Wilson via email or text message. Make sure to include your name and the games you're picking, along with their corresponding priorities.

4. In the event of a tie, the person with the highest single-game priority win will be declared the winner. For example, if two people are tied with a weekly score of 10, the person who won with a priority 5 game will win the tiebreaker.

5. The person with the highest weekly score will win $50 from the pot. In the event that there are multiple weeks with the same highest score, the winner will be determined by a random draw among the tied scores.

6. The overall winner of the pool will be the person who accumulates the most wins throughout the duration of the pool (8/30-11/24). In the event of a tie, the person with the highest weekly score at any point during the pool will be declared the overall winner.

If you have any questions or concerns, feel free to reach out to me or Derek Wilson. Let's make this a fun and competitive pool!

Dear Stacy,

Thank you for your email and for following up on our previous conversation. I appreciate your efforts to move forward with finding a solution to the documentation issues in the UK gas and power markets.

Regarding the proposed call on July 18th, I am available at 10:00 a.m. (NY)/3:00 p.m. (London) and look forward to discussing the matter further with you.

I agree that developing a master master agreement may not be the best approach, and it is worth exploring other options. The goals you outlined - developing documentation that can be used with various energy agreements, is amenable to non-ISDA members, and accommodates physical and financial trades - are sensible and align with our own objectives.

In preparation for the call, I have given some thought to potential solutions and would like to discuss the following: (1) adapting existing ISDA documentation to better suit the UK gas and power markets, (2) creating a new protocol or framework that can be used in conjunction with various energy agreements, and (3) developing a standardised confirmation or annex that can be used to supplement existing agreements.

I look forward to discussing these ideas and hearing your thoughts on the call next week.

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 

Dear Joe,

I do appreciate this point and assumed from the draft that this was the
reasoning.  I could reach the same conclusions if the provision in question
were in essence a cross-default provision.

As an obvious example that you provide, if a CP is suspended from a stock
exchange and this produces a default under another agreement, this should
not necessarily impact the Master Agreement.  Similarly, if a party to
another agreement is obligated (my English colleagues insist the correct
word is "obliged") to furnish a particular document and fails to do so,
thus breaching an affirmative covenant, this alone, arguably, should not
have a serious consequence under the Master Agreement.

The reason I do not concur here is that instead, this provision is in
essence a cross-acceleration, usable only if the other instrument is
actually terminated early for default.  This is already the most lenient
variant of a cross-default.  The considerations should for that reason be
different.  I see acceleration as a much more severe event that in practice
does not occur except in serious circumstances.  At least if it is in a
material amount (perhaps by meeting the cross-default threshold), it
should, in my view, have the same effect regardless of the cause.

Regards

Steve Kruft





                    "Joseph Carrico"
                    <Joseph.Carrico@ib.bankgesell
                    schaft.de>

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Here is the continuation of the text:

Dear Steve,

Thank you for your detailed response. I understand your concerns regarding the provision being a cross-acceleration clause rather than a cross-default provision. I agree that acceleration is a more severe event and typically occurs only in serious circumstances.

However, I still believe that the current drafting provides a reasonable balance between the need to address potential defaults and the risk of unnecessary disruption to the Master Agreement. I would like to discuss this further and explore possible alternatives that could address your concerns.

In particular, I was thinking that we could consider introducing a materiality threshold for the cross-acceleration clause, as you suggested. This could help to ensure that only significant events trigger a default under the Master Agreement.

I would appreciate your thoughts on this proposal. I have cc'd several colleagues on this email, including Michael Wood and Alec Cohen, who may also be able to provide input on this matter.

Best regards,
Joe

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Here is the continuation of the text:

Dear Steve,

Thank you for your detailed response. I understand your concerns regarding the provision being a cross-acceleration clause rather than a cross-default provision. I agree that acceleration is a more severe event that typically occurs only in serious circumstances.

However, I still believe that the provision is necessary to protect the parties to the Master Agreement from potential defaults. As you know, the ISDA Master Agreement is a widely used document in the derivatives industry, and it's essential to ensure that it provides adequate protection for all parties involved.

I'd like to discuss this further with you and explore possible alternatives that could address your concerns. Perhaps we could consider introducing a materiality threshold or other qualifying factors to limit the scope of the cross-acceleration clause.

I'd appreciate your thoughts on this matter, and I'm open to hearing your suggestions.

Best regards,
Joe

Subject: Summer Intern Program




I am a member of the  Houston Bar Association -- Minority Opportunities in the Legal Profession  Committee.  One of the programs we sponsor is a first-year law student  internship program.   This committee meets with the students at all of  the law schools in Houston and asks them to submit their resumes.  We then  provide copies of the resumes to prospective employers for review.  The  employer then selects the students they would like to interview.   

 

Some employers have  the students participate in their permanent hiring programs.  Others  provide this simply as a learning experience for the students.  The  employer determines the length of the internship and the compensation.  Six  weeks is a common period.  This is a great program which has provided  valuable experience to a large number of students for many years.  If your  organization would be interested in participating in this program, or if you  would like more information, please e-mail me or call me.  I appreciate  your consideration of this matter. NCL, Inc. at www.nationalcharityleague.org   will be the umbrella site.  Each  chapter that participates will have a web site under this umbrella. "Schoene, Jim" <Jim.Schoene@neg.pge.com> on 04/06/2001 02:49:12 PM
To:	"'Stephanie.Miller@enron.com'" <Stephanie.Miller@enron.com>
cc:	 

Subject:	RE: PG&E Gas Transmission Quarterly Meeting


I see how you are doing this.  You are using some early estimates of fuel I
gave you.  You know you should not trust me.  We have some better numbers
now.  It turns out that our average fuel usage rate for calendar 2000 was
.0044 % per pipeline mile.  Stretching this out over 612 miles, KG to Malin
yields a rate of 2.69%.  This is just one place to start when examining the
impact of the expansion.  The modeling of the expansion yields an
incremental rate for the 200,000 mcfd of 7.46%.  If you combine those on a
weighted average you will get 3.24%.  The quantities at KG are significantly
higher than in your estimate.  Regardless, these are the numbers we are
currently predicting.  The actuals will be different and perhaps lower as
the operators optimize the system performance.  Who knows.  Also we are
predicting that the pipeline looping will be constructed in advance and put
into service ahead of the compression.  So on a long term basis (like the
first year) the fuel rate will decrease due to the  looping.

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
I just spoke to Rick Borden (Royal's counsel).  We went through some of the 
documentation status and his view on how his documents will look.

He will be providing us today with an Acknowledgement Agreement to be 
executed by Enron.  This will acknowledge the assignment of the BF Equity 
documents as security for its credit obligations and will include the 
increased costs indemnity.  His initial draft will use fairly standard 
increased costs language which we can mark-up as required to make more 
palatable for the accountants.

We discussed generally the acceleration on illegality under the BF Equity 
credit agreement.  Still waiting for direction from the business people on 
this.

He also indicated that he would be drafting the Interest Rate Swap.  The 
non-standard features of this will be the adjustment mechanism which they 
propose will apply on Sched II's coming into the syndicate, and on a 
mandatory rollover from BAs' to prime which would occur if there is no market 
for BAs', if it becomes illegal to issue BAs' or if there is a default in the 
credit agreement where such default emanated from Enron (eg. non-payment).  I 
don't think this has been discussed previously and needs to be considered 
from both the business and accounting side.

Other documents:
His form of opinion will be coming today.
The Bank is doing the fee letter and will be distributing today.
Blakes will distribute our forms of opinions today.

If any of the above is problematic we should follow up with the Bank and/or 
its counsel this morning.

Warren Nishimura
Blake, Cassels & Graydon LLP

Dear Julie,

I don't know if this is in time or not. We just got back from camping 
at Enchanted Rock for the long weekend (the kids were off school 
today).

We had our protest hearing for our set-back application on 9/20
before the Planning Commission. There was one property owner
who protested our application, so we had to go to the public hearing.
The short story is that our application was approved and forwarded 
on to City Council, the last step in the process before Peden is
protected from building closer to the street than the average of the 
existing buildings. We applied from Montrose to Taft on both sides 
of Peden. The Planning & Development Dept. (P&DD) records showed 
that we had 76% in favor, and most of the remaining 24% we couldn't
locate to ask to sign before we turned it in. There were two properties
that we slated to be demolished and converted to new townhouses.
With the new set-back in place, new construction is still allowed and
there are no other restriction placed on properties. We're just one step 
away from being protected for 20 years. The guy who protested said
he was planning to tear down two adjacent single story bungalows 
and build townhouses on the double lot. Mike Yates was the co-
applicant, but there was a lot of support and 20% of the signatories 
showed up at the protest hearing! Sorry if this is disjointed, but
I'm really tired from our backpacking and climbing.

Hope this is in time! Let me know if there are any questions.

Carmen

P.S. We saw your van over in Southhampton a couple weeks ago!

Attached is the "term sheet" with some suggested revisions (identified by

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
I would like more detail about how much margin my open positions require and how the muni's are treated with regard to margin.  The call's I am short are way out of the money.  The only positions that are changing very much in value are the Enron shares I am long and the Enron puts I am short.  I would consider closing those positions.  I would like to put the maximum possible into Muni's.

Phillip

 -----Original Message-----
From: 	"Sandoval, Julieta" <

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
julieta.sandoval@ubspainewebber.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Here is the continuation of the text:

To: Phillip 

Dear Phillip,

Thank you for your email. I have forwarded your request to Steve as you requested. In the meantime, I will provide you with some general information regarding margin requirements for your open positions.

The margin requirements for your open positions are calculated based on the current market value of the securities and the specific requirements for each type of security. For your out-of-the-money short call positions, the margin requirement is relatively low since they are not deeply in-the-money. However, we still require a certain amount of margin to cover potential losses.

Regarding municipal bonds (munis), they are generally considered to be low-risk securities and are often used as collateral for margin loans. The margin requirement for munis is typically lower compared to other types of securities.

As for your Enron shares and short puts, we understand that these positions are experiencing significant price movements. We can discuss the possibility of closing these positions and reallocating the funds to munis.

Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns. Steve will be in touch with you shortly to discuss your account in more detail.

Best regards,
Julieta Sandoval

Dear Phillip,

Thank you for your inquiry regarding the yield on your investment. I understand that you are targeting a 5% yield with an average maturity date of 2020.

You are correct that the current yield on the treasury is 6.25%, but you're also right that the effective yield is reduced due to the tax implications. At a 39% tax bracket, the effective yield would be approximately 3.81% (6.25% * (1 - 0.39)). 

You are considering shifting your investment to municipal bonds (munis) to potentially achieve a higher after-tax yield. This is a valid consideration, as munis are generally tax-exempt, which can result in a higher after-tax return compared to taxable investments like treasuries.

However, before making a decision, it's essential to evaluate the credit quality, liquidity, and diversification implications of investing in munis. While munis can offer attractive after-tax yields, they may also come with different risks, such as credit risk.

To determine the best course of action, let's examine the specifics of the munis you're considering. What is the credit rating, and what is the yield on the munis you're looking at? We can compare these factors to the current treasury

Monthly Newsletter

Let's describe a typical and recurring nightmare for generating unit
operators. It's noon on August 15, temperatures have reached the upper 90s
all week; you're well into your reserve margin to serve native load, when a
unit trips off line. Do you scramble to find replacement power at record

for this event?

lead to restless nights, and understandably so. The financial implications
can be astounding when a unit outage occurs during a period of high spikes
in market prices.

How does this relate to the recruitment and hiring of talent within your
shop? Allow me to bring the story together. What would you do if your top

----- Original Message -----  
From: Teresa Glaze  
To: Lloyd Will  ; Presto, Kevin  
Sent: Friday, October 05, 2001 11:29 PM
Subject: Data for Determination of Voting Rights
RESPONSE DUE TO SERC OFFICE BY OCTOBER  22, 2001  
SERC Engineering Committee Representatives
It is time to determine member voting rights and membership  assessments for the year 2002. Again this year, we will calculate these  percentages based on the formula agreed to at the April 27, 1994 meeting of the  SERC Board of Directors:
    V = 10 ( 1/N) + 30(B/C) + 30(D/E) +  30(F/G)
where,
    V = % of Voting Rights
    N = Total number of Members
    B = Member's previous-year internal peak  demand
    C = Total of factor B for all  Members
    D = Member's generating capacity as of  January 1 of the reporting year
    E = Total of factor D for all  Members
    F = Sum of circuit miles of transmission  times the respective operating voltage for facilities of 69 kV and above as of  December 31 of the previous year
    G = Total of factor F for all  Members
 
The required data are: 
1) Peak Demand for the calendar year 2000, 
2) Total Capacity owned as of January 1, 2001, and  
3) Circuit miles of transmission owned as of December 31,  2000.
As a precaution, remember that 1) generation under joint  ownership, 2) generation belonging to non-members of SERC, and 3) non-utility  generation will have to be coordinated between the involved  parties.
The attached worksheet contains the information that I  have in my database for 2001. 
Please update this worksheet and return to me (

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
tglaze@serc1.org

If we relocate him to Pasadena, he will find his way back! I vote SPCA

-----Original Message-----
From: Craig Lindberg [mailto:

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
craigl@calpine.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
clindberg@cityofpasadena.net]
To: Animal Control Discussion Group
Subject: Re: Fwd: Lost Dog - Max

I understand your concerns, but I strongly disagree with relocating Max to Pasadena. As a representative of the City of Pasadena's Animal Control, I've seen numerous cases where relocated animals have managed to find their way back to their original homes, sometimes covering great distances.

While I appreciate the SPCA's willingness to take Max in, I'm worried that relocating him could cause more harm than good. Max has already formed a bond with his current caregivers, and uprooting him again could lead to further stress and anxiety for the animal.

Instead, I propose that we work with the current caregivers to ensure Max receives the necessary care and attention while efforts are made to locate his original owners. We can also post flyers in the neighborhood, check with local veterinarians, and scan Max for a microchip to try to reunite him with his family.

Let's exhaust all local options before considering relocation. I'd be happy to help coordinate these efforts and provide any necessary support.

Best regards,
Craig Lindberg
Animal Control Officer
City of Pasadena
